summary:
The Allied occupation of Japan lasted from September 1945 until April 1952, the longest occupation of a major power in modern history. Under the supreme authority of General Douglas MacArthur, the occupation undertook a sweeping transformation of Japanese society and government. The key objectives were demilitarization — disbanding Japan's armed forces and conducting war crimes trials — and democratization, restructuring Japanese political, economic, and social institutions along American liberal democratic lines.

The occupation produced a new constitution in 1947, drafted largely by American officials, which renounced war as a sovereign right (Article 9), established a parliamentary democracy, guaranteed extensive civil liberties, and emancipated women. Land reform broke up large estates and redistributed land to tenant farmers, fundamentally altering rural power structures. Major industrial conglomerates (zaibatsu) were dissolved, though many reconstituted in modified forms. The Tokyo War Crimes Tribunal tried and convicted senior military and civilian leaders, executing seven including former Prime Minister Hideki Tojo.

As the [[event:113|Cold War]] intensified, American occupation policy shifted toward rebuilding Japan as a stable ally rather than thoroughly de-industrializing a former enemy. Japan's economy revived sharply, laying the groundwork for the postwar economic miracle. The occupation ended with the signing of the San Francisco Peace Treaty in 1951, though the US-Japan security alliance maintained a substantial American military presence in Japan thereafter.
